evidence_quotes.quote: deception and manipulation of consumers on theernet

paragraphs.text: Preventing deception and manipulation of consumers on theternet requires coordinated action at EU level, as national measures alone cannot be sufficient in today's cross-border digital environment. The growth of digital services, online sales, online platforms and content has resulted in consumers becoming increasingly victims of illegal activities and harmful content online.

section_path: Contribution of the German Animal Welfare Association to the EU consultation on 'Law on digital fairness'

evidence_quotes.quote: often marked by false information about an animal's health, vaccination status, race or origin

paragraphs.text: Uncontrolled online trade in all types of animals opens the door to illegal traders. It is possible to act anonymously online and consumers can be misled in a simple way in elusive minors. By means of putative advertisements, often marked by false information about an animal's health, vaccination status, race or origin, as well as sales, sellers would encourage consumers and urge overwhelmed purchases of animals, which are often found to be illegal in retrospect or involve the sale of animals that are not suitable for private husbandry. These include, for example, many wild species. After the trade, sellers are often out of reach and overwhelmed by animals, as animals from illegal trade are often ill and high unexpected costs arise, and the expectations of rearing oftend not match reality. Due to ignorance and over-- requirement, these animals are not rarely released in shelters or are exposed worst. These activities give rise to massive animal suffering. On the one hand, financial losses and emotional stress also occur on the part of the buyers themselves. Illegal animal traders are also increasingly using such zial media to circumvent rules, taking advantage of bad enforcement and limited proactive measures of online plating. All this undermines the fundamental principle of digital fairness and consumer protection.

Annual evaluations by the German Association of Animal Welfare show that, in a large part of the cases of illegal trade in animals, animals suffer from signs of disease. Among the diseases, there are numerous zoonoses, so illegal trade in animals also endangers the health of other animals and humans.
I German Animal Welfare Association (2025): Illegal pet trade and its impact on German animal shelters – evaluation of known cases from 2024 Evaluation Illegal Heim-Tierhandel 2024.pdf
Page 2 -- Contribution of the German Animal Welfare Association to the EU consultation on the "Legal act" on digital fairness"

The European Commission has also carried out an investigation into the illegal trade in dogs and cats and, in its law, recognises the need to regulate illegal trade in dogs and cats, including online trade, and recognises the need for a regulation on the welfare and traceability of dogs and cats. The commitment to the identification and registration of all dogs and cats and the possibility of verifying this in online sales are measures under the planned Regulation. Contrary to what was previously provided for in the draft, it would also be necessary to have secure seller identification in online sales (for the sale of any animal species) and to verify the identification and control of dogs and cats or, depending on the type of equivalent identification method, even before online, in order to ultimately stop the anonymous offering of animals and curb illegal trade.

evidence_quotes.quote: stricter monitoring of sales advertisements before they are placed online

paragraphs.text: The Digital Services Act mentions illegal animal trafficking, but there is currently no clear policy or strategy to mimic and curb it. There is therefore an urgent need for stricter monitoring of sales advertisements before they are placed online in order to reduce animal suffering, to protect buyers from fraud, misinformation and illegal machining, and to break the structures behind illegal animal trade. Stricter controls to ensure digital fairness in the European Union could therefore help to better protect both consumers and the animals themselves.

The need to curb the illegal trade in animals in the interests of consumer and digital fairness is undisputed, as it has far-reaching implications for public health, safety, animal nature and species protection, as well as significant financial implications for consumers and Member States themselves.

evidence_quotes.quote: under-year-olds are over-averagely exposed to misleading advertising of pets
disturbing content such as animal mistreatment or cruelty

paragraphs.text: Cruelty to animals in social media is seen in a variety of forms. It is often not apparent at first glance to non-school users and is so widely disseminated, which provides financial incentives for content creator and canal operators to produce such content more. In particular, under-year-olds are over-averagely exposed to misleading advertising of pets and in many cases also disturbing content such as animal mistreatment or cruelty, which can cause emotional harm and normalise unethical behaviour. However, the protection of consumers and minors in digital spaces is still insufficient, illustrating the urgency of strong and comprehensive measures.

An international criminal network, which produced pornographic videos with cruelty to animals, was recently uncovered. In these areas, cats, rabbits, mice, guinea pigs, fish and other animals were tortured and killed in a sadistic manner. The revelations provoked great outrage and led to increasingly noisy calls for stricter action and better enforcement. Online platforms and social networks should take more responsibility to prevent the publication, dissemination and access to such content, rather than leaving this task to charitable groups that act against cruelty on line.

Consumers have been and continue to be solely responsible for what content they consume in social media. This has led to the most vulnerable groups. It is imperative to implement the concept of digital trust over-industry to ensure secure data sharing and trustful verification to enable the development of secure, secure, interoperable and sustainable digital trust ecosystems.
The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
